concept.headline: Kotlin has two variable keywords: val (read-only) and var (mutable). The compiler infers types so you rarely need to write them.
concept.explanation: val declares a binding you cannot reassign — once set, it stays. var declares a variable you can reassign at any time. Both support type inference: Kotlin looks at the initial value and determines the type automatically. Prefer val by default; switch to var only when you genuinely need to change the value.
concept.syntax:
val name = "Alice"          // inferred String, read-only
var score = 0               // inferred Int, mutable
score = 10                  // OK — var can be reassigned
// name = "Bob"             // ERROR — val cannot be reassigned

// Explicit types (optional, same result)
val pi: Double = 3.14
var count: Int = 0
keyPoints: val — assign once, never reassign (like Java's final)
var — can be reassigned any number of times
Type inference means you rarely write the type explicitly
Prefer val; use var only when the value must change

demo.intro: Watch how val and var behave as we track a player's name and score.
demo.code:
fun main() {
    val name = "Alice"
    var score = 0
    println("$name: $score")
    score = 10
    println("$name: $score")
}

explanation.what: val name = "Alice" — declaring a read-only String
explanation.why: The player's name won't change during the game, so val is the right choice
explanation.intent: Show that val binds a name to a value permanently
explanation.misconception: val does not mean the object is deeply immutable — a val List can still have mutable contents. It only means this variable binding cannot point to a different object.

explanation.what: var score = 0 — declaring a mutable Int
explanation.why: The score will change as the player earns points, so var is appropriate
explanation.intent: Show that var allows future reassignment
explanation.misconception: You still need to initialize var before using it — Kotlin does not assign a default value like null automatically for local variables

explanation.why: String template $name embeds the value of name, $score embeds score
explanation.intent: Demonstrate string templates that read both variables
explanation.misconception: $name in a string template does NOT call toString() on a String object — it simply inserts the string value. For expressions use ${...}.

explanation.why: The player earned points; we update the mutable variable
explanation.misconception: If score were declared with val, this line would be a compile error — 'Val cannot be reassigned'

explanation.why: score was updated to 10; name is still 'Alice'
explanation.intent: Confirm the reassignment is reflected immediately
explanation.misconception: name did not need to change — val variables can be read any number of times, they just cannot be reassigned

prediction.question:
What happens if you try to reassign a val variable?

val city = "Paris"
city = "London"
options: city is silently ignored and stays 'Paris'
Compile error: Val cannot be reassigned
prediction.explanation: Kotlin catches val reassignment at compile time — the program won't even build. This is one of Kotlin's safety features: immutability violations are errors, not runtime surprises.

explanation.afterDemo: val and var are Kotlin's way of making intent explicit in the type system. When you see val you immediately know this value won't change — no need to search the rest of the code to be sure. var signals 'watch out, this may change'. Prefer val everywhere and only reach for var when mutation is genuinely needed.
keyTakeaways: val = read-only binding: cannot be reassigned after initialization. Use it by default.
var = mutable binding: can be reassigned any number of times.
Type inference reads the initial value — you rarely need to write the type explicitly.

practice.prompt: Declare a val city set to "Tokyo" and a var temperature set to 20. Then update temperature to 25 and print: "Tokyo: 25°C".
practice.hint: Use val for city and var for temperature. Update temperature with a plain assignment, then use a string template to print both.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! city stays 'Tokyo' (val) while temperature changes from 20 to 25 (var).
retryHints: Declare with: val city = "Tokyo" and var temperature = 20
Reassign with: temperature = 25 (no val/var keyword on reassignment)

summary.title: Variables — Complete
takeaways: val is read-only: assign once, never reassign. Prefer it by default.
var is mutable: reassign as needed. Use only when the value genuinely changes.
Type inference removes boilerplate — Kotlin deduces the type from the initial value.
